profile.summary: A junior product designer supports end-to-end product design on small features or well-scoped problems. They usually work with a product manager, engineers, and a more senior designer to turn requirements and research into flows, wireframes, UI, and prototypes. Their ownership is mostly executional, with growing responsibility for rationale, collaboration, and using feedback well.

known_challenges.text: Turning vague product requirements into clear design tasks without over-relying on senior teammates.
Balancing speed with craft when shipping UI work under tight deadlines.
Handling frequent critique without losing confidence or becoming overly attached to first ideas.
Learning the design system while also spotting when a pattern does not quite fit the problem.
Keeping up with product, engineering, and user context across several small pieces of work.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Feedback quality and how they are using critique
recommended_question_themes.why: Junior designers improve fastest when feedback is specific, timely, and easy to apply in the next iteration.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Clarity of scope and what good looks like
recommended_question_themes.why: This role often stalls when the problem, constraints, or expected output are still fuzzy.
They need to grow from making screens to connecting choices back to user needs and product goals.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Working relationship with product and engineering
recommended_question_themes.why: Early-career designers often need help navigating trade-offs, handoff, and day-to-day collaboration.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Energy, morale, and reaction to critique cycles
recommended_question_themes.why: Repeated revisions and comparison with stronger peers can quietly drain confidence and motivation.

terminology.meaning: Basic layout showing structure before visual polish.
Steps a user takes to complete a task.
Clickable model used to test or explain ideas.
Shared components, patterns, and rules for product UI.
Designing so more people can use the product.
Passing design specs and intent to engineers.
Structured feedback on work in progress.
Improving a design through repeated changes.
Limits from tech, business, time, or policy.
Working closely with product, engineering, and others.
Common tool for UI design and collaboration.
Reusable UI building block in a design system.
Clear reason behind a design choice.
Less common scenario that still needs support.
Using AI prompts to explore ideas or copy.

listen_for: They talk mostly about screens and tasks, or they connect work to user and product outcomes.
They mention waiting for feedback a lot, which can signal dependency or unclear support.
They use design system language confidently, or avoid it because they are unsure.
They describe engineering trade-offs with curiosity, or as blockers they do not yet know how to navigate.
They can explain why they changed a design after critique, not just that they changed it.
avoid: Treating them like a fully independent designer on ambiguous work with little guidance.
Giving only taste-based feedback instead of clear reasoning they can learn from.
Assuming quietness in critique means agreement or confidence.
Pushing speed so hard that learning, craft, and reflection disappear.
